summary: In 1994, I spent an academic year studying in Budapest, Hungary, despite not speaking Hungarian or having a related major. Life in Budapest during the early 1990s was tough, with high unemployment and social changes following the fall of Communism. I lived alone in a rented apartment filled with books left by a Hungarian journalist and struggled with limited resources like expensive phone calls and no internet. Despite the hardships, I found solace in the British Council library and English bookstores, where I discovered Granta magazine, which became a great comfort during my time there.

Hungary in the early 1990s was undergoing a difficult transition after the fall of Communism, which many called 'the change in the system.' The country faced high unemployment and the loss of subsidized cultural and social benefits, which hit many Hungarians hard. I was there in 1994 on a scholarship, witnessing these changes firsthand while living in Budapest. Although I wasn’t Hungarian, I experienced the bleakness of the period and the resilience of its people, including the Hungarian journalist who owned my apartment.

The fall of Communism in Hungary around the early 1990s was a pivotal event that shaped the country’s social and economic landscape. While many Westerners celebrated it as liberation, many Hungarians referred to it as 'the change in the system,' which brought hardship like high unemployment and loss of social benefits. I lived in Budapest during this time and saw how the transition affected everyday life, making it a bleak period for many. This backdrop influenced my experience as a foreign student trying to navigate life in Hungary during those years.

In Hungary during the early 1990s, Russian was no longer a compulsory language in schools, which was a significant change after the Communist era. Former Russian teachers were retraining to teach English, and organizations like the Peace Corps stepped in to fill the demand for English teachers. This shift was part of the broader social changes I witnessed while living in Budapest in 1994. Although I didn’t teach Russian or speak it, the decline of Russian language study was a notable part of the cultural transition at the time.

During the early 1990s in Hungary, the Peace Corps played an important role by helping to fill the gap left by former Russian teachers who were retraining to teach English. They, along with other English as a Foreign Language teachers, were crucial in meeting the demand for English education after Russian was dropped from the curriculum. While I wasn’t part of the Peace Corps, I was aware of their presence and contribution during my time in Budapest in 1994. Their work was part of the larger cultural shifts happening in Hungary after the fall of Communism.

While living in Budapest in 1994, I discovered the British Council library, which became an invaluable resource for me. I managed to get a lending card there, which gave me access to English language books and materials that were otherwise hard to find. This was especially important since I had limited access to university resources and no internet. The British Council library, along with its American counterpart, helped me cope with the loneliness and isolation I felt during that challenging year.

I first discovered Granta in an English language bookstore in Budapest during my 1994 academic year there, and I immediately loved its format. Each issue was themed and included a mix of poetry, photography, short fiction, and personal essays, which provided me with literary comfort during a difficult time. Granta became my companion through months of loneliness, offering a connection to a wider language community and a source of cheer and commiseration. Even now, nearly 25 years later, I still subscribe to Granta and find joy in reading new issues or revisiting those first ones I found in Budapest.
